Ryan Schwiebert (Sep 16 2024 at 03:39):

I've been looking at the last exercise in the section. It generalizes, a bit, the lemma you alluded to earlier @John Baez :

If F:A→BF: \mathcal A\to \mathcal BF:A→B and F⊣GF\dashv GF⊣G, and C\mathcal CC is any other category, then you have a functor like F∗:[B,C]→[A,C]F^\ast :[\mathcal B,\mathcal C]\to [\mathcal A,\mathcal C]F∗:[B,C]→[A,C]. Show G∗⊣F∗G^\ast\dashv F^\astG∗⊣F∗ (hint: use 2.2.5, which says

Given functors F:A→B:GF:\mathcal A\to \mathcal B: GF:A→B:G there's a 1-1 correspondence between a) adjunctions with F on the left, G on the right; b) pairs of natural transformations 1→GF1\to GF1→GF, FG→1FG\to 1FG→1 satisfying the triangle identities. (Some notational details omitted.)

The action of F∗F^\astF∗ on the objects was given (the obvious one) as F∗(Y):=Y∘FF^\ast(Y):=Y\circ FF∗(Y):=Y∘F. From that, I think I found the right action on arrows: given an arrow α:Y→Y′\alpha: Y\to Y'α:Y→Y′, I think F∗(α)F^\ast(\alpha)F∗(α)'s AAA component should be α\alphaα's F(A)F(A)F(A) component. Using this, I found F∗F^\astF∗ to act as a functor.

When considering G∗F∗(Y)=Y∘F∘GG^\ast F^\ast (Y)=Y\circ F\circ GG∗F∗(Y)=Y∘F∘G, I felt sure the fact that F∘G≅1F\circ G\cong 1F∘G≅1 must yield Y∘F∘G≅YY\circ F\circ G\cong YY∘F∘G≅Y, so that G∗F∗≅1G^\ast F^\ast\cong 1G∗F∗≅1. This took a lot of thinking, but I think I've established that YϵY\epsilonYϵ (where epsilon is the counit for F⊣GF\dashv GF⊣G) witnesses this (this is whiskering again?) Am I on the right track?

I have to consider the triangle identities next. I'm really feeling the burn mentally from reasoning about functors on functor categories.

John Baez (Sep 16 2024 at 19:15):

Where are you getting F∘G≅1F \circ G \cong 1F∘G≅1 from? The exercise just stated that FFF is left adjoint to GGG. This implies you have natural transformations F∘G⇒1F \circ G \Rightarrow 1F∘G⇒1, 1⇒G∘F1 \Rightarrow G \circ F1⇒G∘F obeying the triangle identities... but these natural transformations don't need to be isomorphisms.

Ryan Schwiebert (Sep 17 2024 at 03:44):

John Baez said:

Where are you getting F∘G≅1F \circ G \cong 1F∘G≅1 from?

Sigh I can see it only takes a couple of days for these sorts of hallucinations set in. Signs of desperate grasping for footholds.

With that correction in mind, I've got FG→1FG\to 1FG→1, 1→GF1\to GF1→GF, and I need 1→F∗G∗1\to F^\ast G^\ast1→F∗G∗. From F∗G∗(Φ)=Φ∘G∘FF^\ast G^\ast(\Phi)=\Phi\circ G\circ FF∗G∗(Φ)=Φ∘G∘F, I think the way forward must be something related to the unit for adjunction (F,G,η,ϵ)(F,G,\eta,\epsilon)(F,G,η,ϵ), right? Can't we horizontally compose the identity transformation on Φ\PhiΦ with ϵ\epsilonϵ to get a natural transformation Φ→Φ∘G∘F\Phi\to\Phi \circ G\circ FΦ→Φ∘G∘F?

But now my head is splitting as to whether or not this means 1[A,C]→F∗G∗1_{[\mathcal A,\mathcal C]}\to F^\ast G^\ast1[A,C]​→F∗G∗... is each horizontal composition using Φ\PhiΦ the component of a natural transformation in [A,C][\mathcal A,\mathcal C][A,C]? If so I haven't gotten to naturality yet... And _then_ there's something something about the triangle equations to figure out.

Am I missing some observations that are supposed to make the computations simple? I was under the impression I shouldn't be so bogged down by now.

John Baez (Sep 17 2024 at 04:18):

Has Leinster's book explained the ways you can compose functors and natural transformations:

  1. composition of functors
  2. vertical and horizontal composition of natural transformations
  3. left and right whiskering of a natural transformation by a functor

and the rules these operations obey? If you know these, I think this exercise is not too terribly hard: everything we need to get an adjunction between F∗F^\astF∗ and G∗G^\astG∗ should come from the corresponding bits of the adjunction between FFF and GGG.

John Baez (Sep 17 2024 at 04:22):

If you don't know this stuff, then you're in the position of having to make a bit of it up. It seems like that's what you're doing. In that case I'll just emphasize what I just said: each of the 4 features of the adjunction between F∗F^\astF∗ and G∗G^\astG∗ (the unit, the counit, and the two triangle identities) should come from the corresponding feature of the adjunction between FFF and GGG.

John Baez (Sep 17 2024 at 04:22):

I believe there's a high-powered way to do this exercise which makes it super-quick, but it uses a bit of 2-category theory - namely, any 2-functor maps adjunctions to adjunctions.

Ryan Schwiebert (Sep 17 2024 at 11:32):

For sure I have the definitions of the first two. I understand the third as being a special case of horizontal composition.

John Baez said:

the rules these operations obey?

I know about the interchange law but haven't applied it anywhere yet... it looks very relevant for something like this, here. I'm happy to keep working on it on my own. If my toolbox (1-3 and the interchange law) is missing something obvious for this problem, please let me know.

John Baez (Sep 17 2024 at 15:22):

Good! It sounds like you know all the rules. I hope you know how to draw Whiskering is indeed a special case of horizontal composition, so you can derive its rules from those for horizontal composition, but I believe it's so important for this problem that it's worth special attention.

For example:

  • F∗F^\astF∗ is composition with F ⁣:A→BF \colon \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}F:A→B: for any functor H ⁣:B→CH \colon \mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{C}H:B→C we have

F∗(H)=H∘F F^\ast(H) = H \circ F F∗(H)=H∘F

  • G∗G^\astG∗ is composition with G:B→AG: \mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{A}G:B→A: for any functor K ⁣:A→CK \colon \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{C}K:A→C we have

G∗(K)=K∘G G^\ast(K) = K \circ G G∗(K)=K∘G

  • F∗∘G∗F^\ast \circ G^\astF∗∘G∗ is composition with G∘FG \circ FG∘F:

(F∗∘G∗)(H)=H∘G∘F (F^\ast \circ G^\ast) (H) = H \circ G \circ F (F∗∘G∗)(H)=H∘G∘F

  • the counit ϵ∗ ⁣:F∗G∗⇒1\epsilon^\ast \colon F^\ast G^\ast \Rightarrow 1ϵ∗:F∗G∗⇒1 is whiskering with the unit ϵ ⁣:1⇒GF\epsilon \colon 1 \Rightarrow G F ϵ:1⇒GF

John Baez (Sep 17 2024 at 15:26):

I said each item of the adjunction you seek, G∗⊣F∗G^\ast \dashv F^\astG∗⊣F∗, came from the corresponding item of the adjunction you have, F⊣GF \dashv GF⊣G. But I was forgetting the contravariance: the counit of the adjunction you seek comes from the unit of the adjunction you have and the unit of the adjunction you seek comes from the counit of the adjunction you have. The triangle identities probably also get switched around in this way.

John Baez (Sep 17 2024 at 15:29):

To me it's very important to draw how the counit ϵ∗\epsilon^\astϵ∗ and unit η∗\eta^\astη∗ arise via whiskering from ϵ\epsilonϵ and η\etaη, because then - I believe - the whole proof can be carried out using pictures.

So one question is whether you know how to draw categories (dots), functors (arrows between dots), and natural transformations (globes between arrows).

John Baez (Sep 17 2024 at 15:34):

For an example of what I'm talking about, see the picture of the interchange law here.

Ryan Schwiebert (Sep 18 2024 at 02:55):

I can tell the name was chosen with something in mind, but I feel like there's an inside joke nobody's telling me about or something. The diagrams like the ones we're discussing don't seem to explain the term, but I know there are alternative forms of such diagrams that might.

John Baez (Sep 18 2024 at 03:02):

A 2-morphism looks like a mouth, and we can attach 1-morphisms that look like whiskers on either side:

  • the counit ϵ∗:F∗G∗⇒1 is whiskering with the unit ϵ:1⇒GF

With G∗⊣F∗G^\ast\dashv F^\astG∗⊣F∗ we'd expect the counit to be G∗F∗→1G^\ast F^\ast\to 1G∗F∗→1, corresponding to H∘F∘GH\circ F\circ GH∘F∘G to be related to the counit F∘G→1F\circ G\to 1F∘G→1, right? I think the reversal of the letters in the adjunct relationship cancels out the reversal of the letters when they are applied.

Ryan Schwiebert (Sep 18 2024 at 03:10):

like whiskers on either side

John Baez (Sep 18 2024 at 03:13):

This really is the origin of the term whiskering - not the picture of Dali, but the idea that a whisker is a line segment protruding from a bigon:

What I'm stuck on is showing that if Φ′\Phi'Φ′ is another functor and α:Φ→Φ′\alpha : \Phi\to \Phi'α:Φ→Φ′, the rest of the diagram commutes, so that ϵ∗\epsilon^\astϵ∗ is a natural transformation. Thus I'm working on showing α∘Φϵ=Φ′ϵ∘G∗F∗(α)\alpha\circ \Phi\epsilon = \Phi'\epsilon\circ G^\ast F^\ast(\alpha)α∘Φϵ=Φ′ϵ∘G∗F∗(α).

That led me to consider applying the interchange law to (αF∘G)∘(Φ′ϵ)(\alpha F\circ G)\circ(\Phi'\epsilon)(αF∘G)∘(Φ′ϵ), but when I did that I thought I was getting α∗ϵ\alpha\ast \epsilonα∗ϵ which puzzles me because a) it no longer references either of the Phi's and b) because I'm not completely sure how to link α∘Φϵ\alpha\circ \Phi\epsilonα∘Φϵ with it.

Can you always write α=α∗1[B,C]\alpha=\alpha \ast 1_{[\mathcal B,\mathcal C]}α=α∗1[B,C]​? If so, it seems like interchange applies to α∘Φϵ\alpha\circ \Phi\epsilonα∘Φϵ too and that gets you α∗ϵ\alpha\ast\epsilonα∗ϵ maybe. John Baez (Sep 26 2024 at 20:11):

Ryan Schwiebert said:

I've made some strides thinking in terms of the diagrams, but I'm still blocked. Unless I misunderstood something earlier, the candidate for the counit of G∗⊣F∗G^\ast\dashv F^\astG∗⊣F∗ is ϵ∗\epsilon^\astϵ∗ whose components, for each functor Φ:B→C\Phi : \mathcal B\to \mathcal CΦ:B→C are given by whiskering Φϵ\Phi\epsilonΦϵ, where ϵ\epsilonϵ is the counit for F⊣GF\dashv GF⊣G.

That sounds right.

If I were talking to you about this in person, I'd want all these entities like Φϵ\Phi \epsilonΦϵ to be drawn as 2-dimensional diagrams, and the things you want to showto also be drawn as diagrams. It's far harder to think about sort of argument using 1-dimensional strings of symbols. Unfortunately it's a pain to draw diagrams here (though some people use their cell phone to upload hand-drawn pictures, which is probably the fastest way).

For example, when you say Φϵ\Phi \epsilonΦϵ, I'm imagining an arrow Φ\PhiΦ whiskered onto a triangle, namely ϵ\epsilonϵ, which has one edge labelled 111 on top and two edges labelled FFF and GGG bottom. Does that match your picture?

(I hope you know you can draw a natural transformation ϵ:1→GF\epsilon : 1 \to G Fϵ:1→GF as a triangle in this way, though there are other equally fine drawing styles.)

What I'm stuck on is showing that if Φ′\Phi'Φ′ is another functor and α:Φ→Φ′\alpha : \Phi\to \Phi'α:Φ→Φ′, the rest of the diagram commutes, so that ϵ∗\epsilon^\astϵ∗ is a natural transformation.

By the way it's really useful to write natural transformations as ⇒\Rightarrow⇒ so we can instantly distinguish them from functors, drawn as →\to→. Natural transformations are arrows going between arrows, so they are 2-dimensional, so a bunch of us denote them by double arrows.

Thus I'm working on showing α∘Φϵ=Φ′ϵ∘G∗F∗(α)\alpha\circ \Phi\epsilon = \Phi'\epsilon\circ G^\ast F^\ast(\alpha)α∘Φϵ=Φ′ϵ∘G∗F∗(α).

Okay, now let me take time to translate this into a picture.

John Baez (Sep 26 2024 at 20:15):

By the way, I forgot to reply to this:

Ryan Schwiebert said:

John Baez said:

  • the counit ϵ∗:G∗F∗⇒1\epsilon^\ast: G^\ast F^\ast \Rightarrow 1ϵ∗:G∗F∗⇒1 is whiskering with the unit ϵ:1→GF\epsilon: 1 \to G Fϵ:1→GF.

Isn't it unit to unit, counit to counit? With G∗⊣F∗G^\ast\dashv F^\astG∗⊣F∗ we'd expect the counit to be G∗F∗→1G^\ast F^\ast\to 1G∗F∗→1, corresponding to H∘F∘GH\circ F\circ GH∘F∘G to be related to the counit F∘G→1F\circ G\to 1F∘G→1, right? I think the reversal of the letters in the adjunct relationship cancels out the reversal of the letters when they are applied.

Quite possibly... I'll draw this stuff and see what's up.

John Baez (Sep 26 2024 at 20:36):

Okay, as a diagram this picture:

α∘Φϵ=Φ′ϵ∘G∗F∗(α)\alpha\circ \Phi\epsilon = \Phi'\epsilon\circ G^\ast F^\ast(\alpha)α∘Φϵ=Φ′ϵ∘G∗F∗(α).

looks like this:

(You can click on the picture to make it bigger.)

Each side of the equation here is a vertical composite of 2 natural transformations. Each natural transformation is the whiskering of a natural transformation by a functor.

When I draw two functors as parallel lines or arcs and leave one unlabeled, it's the same as the one parallel to it.

John Baez (Sep 26 2024 at 20:39):

The equation is true by interchange law. Both sides are different ways of writing this natural transformation:

Ryan Schwiebert (Sep 27 2024 at 11:31):

@John Baez :astonished: So I did get it right! Well, that's encouraging... now to invest some time in the triangle equalities...

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 03 2024 at 21:58):

OK, I think I got one half of the triangle identity for G∗⊣F∗G^\ast\dashv F^\astG∗⊣F∗:

IMG_0720 2.HEIC

One 'eureka' for me was figuring out that (for Φ\PhiΦ, GGG as in the pic) that (Φ∘G)ϵ=Φ(Gϵ)(\Phi\circ G)\epsilon=\Phi(G\epsilon)(Φ∘G)ϵ=Φ(Gϵ) (the second one is consecutive whiskering.) After that, the diagrams pointed the way to combine the two things I was to verify.

I can't tell you guys how much I've learned struggling with this handful of problems. I'm really glad I've got the opportunity to do it and I'm thankful for your hints. Now... if only I would gain some velocity :wink:

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 03 2024 at 22:01):

Ugh, for whatever reason, the image closes a fraction of a second after I open it. In my zulip client, anyhow. Here's a jpeg export:
IMG_0720 2-1727992706203.jpg

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 03 2024 at 22:06):

I also meant to ask if my intuition that F′(αF)=(F′α)FF'(\alpha F) = (F'\alpha)FF′(αF)=(F′α)F holds (associativity of whiskering on the sides of a natural transformation.) I tried to prove this at one point and wound up with a cube of commutative squares, but not completely reassured I was right. That would be important in the proof above where I wrote ΦηG\Phi\eta GΦηG without parens...

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 03 2024 at 22:14):

Actually I got the cube trying to prove associativity of horizontal composition (which would imply associativity of whiskering around a natural transformation.)

John Baez (Oct 03 2024 at 23:23):

Nice, I'm glad you're working ht

These exercises take time to do, but that's fine, because you need to learn all sorts of techniques and ideas to do them... and that's actually the main point of the exercises!

Ryan Schwiebert said:

I also meant to ask if my intuition that F′(αF)=(F′α)FF'(\alpha F) = (F'\alpha)FF′(αF)=(F′α)F holds (associativity of whiskering on the sides of a natural transformation.)

Yes, it's true. Whiskering with a functor is horizontal composition with an identity natural transformation, and horizontal composition of natural transformations is associative (as you should check), so whiskering is associative in this manner.

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 13 2024 at 02:52):

Since there was already a question at math.stackexchange.com for the "what are adjunctions between groups considered as one-element categories" I posted my answer there: https://math.stackexchange.com/a/4983823/29335

Valid and in the spirit of the chapter, I hope...

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 14 2024 at 11:33):

Looks like someone did not like it, anyhow :(

John Baez (Oct 14 2024 at 16:16):

Maybe because it didn't reach the final punchy conclusion: any adjunction between groups considered as one-element categories is an equivalence and thus gives an isomorphism between these groups.

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Oct 14 2024 at 19:16):

Indeed, I saw your answer before it vanished, and I think the problem was a stylistic one: answers on math.se are expected to be definitive, not inconclusive. Don't take it personally!

John Baez (Oct 14 2024 at 19:28):

That expectation goes away for questions that are so hard nonody can answer them conclusively... but here someone already had.

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 15 2024 at 02:53):

@Morgan Rogers (he/him) I've had a 12.5 year 150k+ career at math.stackexchange, I think I'll be ok. I definitely have had worse answers before. I took it down because I realized, after rereading, that I had skipped the OP's solution and it covered much of the same ground as I did, and failed to cover the OP's last question. I can see why someone would downvote that...

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 15 2024 at 02:56):

I was careful to read the existing answers, which I noticed did not make any use of the comma category approach, so I wrongly assumed that bringing it up would be novel. Sadly, this is another episode of "stupid things to miss" for me.

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 15 2024 at 03:06):

John Baez said:

didn't reach the final punchy conclusion: any adjunction between groups considered as one-element categories is an equivalence and thus gives an isomorphism between these groups.

Well, I thought I did _that_ at least. The answer included

Recalling that functors between groups are just group homomorphisms, this is saying that 𝑄 is an isomorphism of H→GH→GH→G. Observing that natural transformations are natural isomorphisms in this case, we can argue 𝑃≅𝑄−1𝑃≅𝑄^{−1}P≅Q−1 (isomorphic in the category of functors on GGG) and so 𝑃𝑃P is also an isomorphism.

But I do feel like I failed to reach the finish line on the answer anyway. Saying merely "an adjunction between groups is an equivalence" feels really trivial to me... I had already asked and answered that question for myself during the section on units/counits.

That can't be the whole punchline, can it? Stopping there feels like it is missing something that was intended to be discovered about the current section (comma categories.) The fact the exercise appears in this chapter feels too intentional to ignore.

John Baez (Oct 15 2024 at 04:53):

Well, I thought I did _that_ at least. The answer included...

Okay, sorry - I didn't even notice that part!

John Baez (Oct 15 2024 at 04:56):

That can't be the whole punchline, can it?

I don't know what else there is. One could ask about going backwards, turning an isomorphism of groups into an adjunction between the corresponding 1-object categories, but that's even easier.

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Oct 15 2024 at 10:15):

Ryan Schwiebert said:

Morgan Rogers (he/him) I've had a 12.5 year 150k+ career at math.stackexchange, I think I'll be ok.

Hahaha I didn't register that at all, makes my previous comment seem very condescending, sorry :sweat_smile:

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 15 2024 at 14:52):

I think I'm happy for now with the group question. Thanks both for helping with the feedback. I need some sanity checking on my approach for the next one.

Given Proposition: G:B→AG:\mathcal B \to\mathcal AG:B→A has a left adjoint iff (A⇒G)(A\Rightarrow G)(A⇒G) has an initial element for every object AAA of A\mathcal AA.

Exercise: State the dual of the proposition above. How would you prove it?

I have a feeling it shouldn't require re-arguing the proofs in the book all dually.

After thinking about it, I think there are few relevant lemmas: (I hope the ad hoc notation isn't too bad. It takes me a while to find the right LaTeX\LaTeXLATE​X)

In what follows, F:A→BF:\mathcal A\to\mathcal BF:A→B and G:B→AG:\mathcal B\to\mathcal AG:B→A

Lemma 0: FFF is equivalently a functor F:Aop→BopF:\mathcal A^{op}\to\mathcal B^{op}F:Aop→Bop

Lemma 1: Saying A:F⊣G:B\mathcal A: F\dashv G :\mathcal BA:F⊣G:B is an adjunction is the same as saying Bop:G⊣F:Aop\mathcal B^{op}: G\dashv F :\mathcal A^{op}Bop:G⊣F:Aop is an adjunction, after viewing FFF and GGG with Lemma 0.

Lemma 2: For comma categories, we have (P⇒Q)op=(Q⇒P)(P\Rightarrow Q)^{op}=(Q\Rightarrow P)(P⇒Q)op=(Q⇒P).

With those things in mind, it quickly follows that FFF has a right adjoint iff (F⇒B)(F\Rightarrow B)(F⇒B) has a terminal object for every object BBB of B\mathcal BB, right?

On a side, note, 2.3.10-2.3.12 are all very fun!

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 22 2024 at 03:24):

IMG_0756 2.jpg

This one made me do a double take. I must not be understanding something correctly. So for X=NX=\mathbb NX=N, apparently the thing I think of as a sequence in N\mathbb NN that goes 1,2,1,3,1,4,1,5…1,2,1,3,1,4,1,5\ldots1,2,1,3,1,4,1,5… is not of the form predicted here, via a function r:N→Nr:\mathbb N\to \mathbb Nr:N→N such that x0=1x_0=1x0​=1 and xn+1=r(xn)x_{n+1}=r(x_n)xn+1​=r(xn​).

I'm comfortable with sequences as functions with domain N\mathbb NN and codomain in a set XXX. And I can understand iteration of an endomorphism of a set. But the way these two things are being mixed together here perplexes me...

John Baez (Oct 22 2024 at 03:29):

Do you have a specific question? You may be getting confused by taking X=NX = \mathbb{N}X=N - it's often confusing to choose an example where two typically distinct things happen to be the same. Try instead X=RX = \mathbb{R}X=R, and consider the sequence xnx_nxn​ of real numbers with

x0=2x_0 = 2x0​=2
xn+1=exnx_{n+1} = e^{x_n}xn+1​=exn​

John Baez (Oct 22 2024 at 03:36):

The universal property of the natural numbers guarantees that such a sequence exists.

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Oct 22 2024 at 09:55):

The exercise is not saying that all sequences arise from endomorphisms!

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Oct 22 2024 at 09:57):

It's saying that a point and an endomorphism uniquely determine a sequence, not the other way around :)

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 22 2024 at 11:33):

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

It's saying that a point and an endomorphism uniquely determine a sequence, not the other way around :)

Yes, this was exactly what I was seeing, and it was not what I was expecting. I read "let's talk about sequences... N is special to sequences" and was prepared for an intimate relationship of the two (somehow beyond the description already had, as a function with domain N.)

But of course, I read right through recursive without realizing that was the main focus. A category of recursive sequences and not sequences in general. I haven't really thought about them since undergrad...

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 22 2024 at 11:46):

John Baez said:

Do you have a specific question?

It was formerly Is the intended topic sequences or not?

It says "A crucial property of sequences is that they can be defined recursively. [etc description]" Immediately I thought "How is that description a property of sequences? 1,2,1,3,... doesn't have that form..."

I think I wouldn't have tripped on it if it had said "a crucial type of sequences are those defined recursively..."

Tom Leinster (Oct 22 2024 at 14:32):

Thanks @Ryan Schwiebert , that's a good point. I should have said something like "some sequences can be defined recursively" instead. Rereading what I wrote, I can see how it gives the false impression that all sequences arise in the way described.

John Baez (Oct 22 2024 at 16:58):

Aha, yes. This is one of those subtle math writing points. "Can be" can be used to mean "can sometimes be", but it can also be used to mean "can always be", so it can be dangerous to use this simple phrase unadorned.

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Oct 22 2024 at 21:55):

:canned_food::bee:

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 23 2024 at 00:55):

@Tom Leinster Hey hey! Nice to see you here! If you run across any of my moping above please rest assured it's just part of my learning process and that I really am enjoying the book. Finally I'm learning in such a way that things are sticking.

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 24 2024 at 01:34):

My best description of that category in the exercise in which (N,0,s)(\mathbb N, 0, s)(N,0,s) is initial (sss is the successor function on N\mathbb NN):

Let Set∗Set_\astSet∗​ denote the category of pointed sets, and U:Set∗→SetU:Set_\ast\to SetU:Set∗​→Set be the forgetful functor. First form the comma category C=(U⇒U)\mathcal C=(U\Rightarrow U)C=(U⇒U). The objects are set arrows f:A→Af:A\to Af:A→A and a morphism g:B→Bg:B\to Bg:B→B is a pointed-set arrow hhh that is an arrow that's doubled to (h,h)(h,h)(h,h) to be a morphism in the comma category. So it's a special subcategory of the comma category.

That's the best phrasing I could think of. I came across the term inserter category while I was reading about comma categories... this is an example, no?

It's superficially similar to the image of the diagonal functor, except that the objects aren't just pairs (A,A)(A,A)(A,A), you can shove any set arrow between them and you get a different object. But the arrows being a copied pair of one arrow makes it look very similar.

Todd Trimble (Oct 24 2024 at 03:44):

This isn't quite right; the comma category would have for its objects triples (X,Y,f:UX→UY)(X, Y, f: UX \to UY)(X,Y,f:UX→UY) where X,YX, YX,Y are pointed sets. The XXX and YYY could be two different sets, whereas the AAA that you're discussing is a single set.

You're not far off in your thinking, but comma categories or inserters would be more elaborate than is necessary. I don't want to give away the answer, but since objects involve just a single set AAA, as you say, an object might be describable as "a set AAA together with blah-de-blah".

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 25 2024 at 02:58):

Todd Trimble said:

The X and Y could be two different sets, whereas the A that you're discussing is a single set.

Of course, but that's what the subcategory bit takes care of. It's the subcategory of those triples having objects (X,Y,f)(X,Y,f)(X,Y,f) where X=YX=YX=Y and arrows (f,g)(f,g)(f,g) where f=gf=gf=g.

Todd Trimble said:

comma categories or inserters would be more elaborate than is necessary.

I don't think the link to comma categories is unreasonable, though. I can imagine three levels of answer:
1) An elementary description, which I feel is little more than what the problem statement already outlines
2) Recognizing it as a special subcategory of a comma category, a topic introduced in the section preceding the section of the exercise.
3) Calling it an inserter category, which is not discussed in the book at all

Considering the audience and context, 3) is obviously unreasonable as a solution (I just brought it up because I ran into it.) Answering with 1) would be of course correct, but the proximity and similarity to comma categories seemed like a big hint to "do better!"

I really quite enjoyed the process of figuring out the functor and categories to use for the comma category, anyhow.

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 25 2024 at 03:06):

I just looked it up... these are what the call "first-order recurrence relations"? I think i can imagine how it might generalize to n-th order recurrences...

Todd Trimble (Oct 25 2024 at 04:29):

This is what I was correcting:

First form the comma category C=(U⇒U)C=(U\Rightarrow U)C=(U⇒U). The objects are set arrows f:A→Af:A\to Af:A→A and a morphism g:B→Bg:B\to Bg:B→B is a pointed-set arrow hhh that is an arrow that's doubled to (h,h)(h,h)(h,h) to be a morphism in the comma category.

Anyway, yes, you could describe it as a subcategory of a comma category.

I just looked it up... these are what the call "first-order recurrence relations"?

I suppose, but it raises the question "how do you define a first-order recurrence relation?"

What would be your answer in the style of 1)?

Ryan Schwiebert (Oct 26 2024 at 16:29):

@Todd Trimble For 1) Just something like "objects are pairs (A,f)(A,f)(A,f) where AAA is an object of Set∗Set_\astSet∗​ and fff is an arrow from Set(A,A)Set(A, A)Set(A,A) (omission of ∗\ast∗ is intentional) and a morphism from (A,f)→(B,g)(A,f)\to (B,g)(A,f)→(B,g) is an arrow hhh in Set∗(A,B)Set_\ast(A,B)Set∗​(A,B) such that h∘f=g∘hh\circ f = g\circ hh∘f=g∘h.

Ryan Schwiebert (Nov 01 2024 at 19:03):

Sanity check, since this type of question has given me problems before:

Let O:Cat→Set\mathcal O:Cat\to SetO:Cat→Set be the functor sending a small category to its set of objects. Exhibit a chain of adjunctions C⊣D⊣O⊣IC\dashv D\dashv \mathcal O \dashv IC⊣D⊣O⊣I.

This time I think I found the right candidates. I think DDD is the functor that turns a set into a small category by making it the discrete category on SSS (in other words, a preorder where distinct elements are incomparable), and III makes an "indiscrete" category on SSS by furnishing one arrow between every two objects (a preorder where every pair of elements is comparable.)

The part to pin down is CCC. If I'm right about DDD, I can see that its purpose is to furnish us a way to group objects of a small category AAA so that we can produce functors from A→D(I)A\to D(I)A→D(I). Objects must be grouped so that members of separate groups do not have arrows between.

If you consider the binary relation aRa′aRa'aRa′ to mean that there's an arrow in category AAA between aaa and a′a'a′, then symmetrize this relationship, then take the transitive closure, I think it should be an equivalence relation finally. This set of equivalence classes should be C(A)C(A)C(A), and any function out of it furnishes a way to map objects of AAA to objects in D(S)D(S)D(S). (I omit the details for now for brevity but I can say more if needed.) I just wonder if this sounds like the right path.

Ryan Schwiebert (Nov 01 2024 at 19:07):

I haven't been rigorous enough about the idea for the equivalence relation. In fact, I'm betting once I get to some category theoretic exercises on relations things might become clearer. Symmetrization and transitive closure are probably functors on relations.

Is it at least true that one can start with a transitive and reflexive relation, then symmetrize it, then take transitive closure, and conclude the final result is an equivalence relation?

Josselin Poiret (Nov 01 2024 at 21:19):

Ryan Schwiebert said:

Is it at least true that one can start with a transitive and reflexive relation, then symmetrize it, then take transitive closure, and conclude the final result is an equivalence relation?

yes.

In general, you would more directly say that you define a b a ~ b a b iff. there exists a path a→a1←a2→⋯→b a \to a_1 \leftarrow a_2 \to \cdots \to b a→a1​←a2​→⋯→b.

John Baez (Nov 01 2024 at 21:35):

@Ryan Schwiebert asked:

Is it at least true that one can start with a transitive and reflexive relation, then symmetrize it, then take transitive closure, and conclude the final result is an equivalence relation?

Yes. It's easy to check that:

  • the symmetrization of any reflexive relation is reflexive,
  • the transitive closure of a reflexive relation is reflexive,
  • the transitive closure of a symmetric relation is symmetric.

So when you take any reflexive relation, then symmetrize it, then take its transitive closure, you get an equivalence relation.

We can also 'reflexivize' any relation in the obvious way.

I believe that if you take any relation RRR, then reflexivize it, then symmetrize it, then take its transitive closure, you get the smallest equivalence relation containing RRR.

You can also just say "take the smallest equivalence relation containing RRR". This exists because the intersection of a collection of equivalence relations is an equivalence relation: it's the smallest equivalence relations containing RRR.

Ryan Schwiebert (Nov 02 2024 at 02:30):

Yeah I guess if I already believe in something like the transitive closure, then I should believe in "equivalence class closure" already. I just never heard it verbalized!

Ryan Schwiebert (Nov 02 2024 at 02:33):

Josselin Poiret said:

In general, you would more directly say that you define a b iff. there exists a path a→a1​←a2​→⋯→b.

That's appealing too. But also it seems we should include such sequences beginning or ending with arrows pointing in opposite directions. I guess you could just say "such a sequence can be extended to one of the form given above: if it starts with a←ba\leftarrow ba←b just shim in an identity a→a←ba\rightarrow a\leftarrow ba→a←b and likewise if necessary for a ←\leftarrow← on the far right."

Ryan Schwiebert (Nov 02 2024 at 02:37):

At any rate, it's exciting to think I got the adjunction chain by myself for once :)

Ryan Schwiebert (Nov 27 2024 at 04:32):

Well I'm back for exercise 4.3.13:
Let A\mathscr AA be a locally small category, and XXX be a presheaf on A\mathscr AA. If X(A)X(A)X(A) has a universal element, show directly (not using the Yoneda lemma) that X≅HAX\cong H_AX≅HA​, the representable functor on the object A∈AA\in \mathscr AA∈A.

I quickly caught on that the universal element property lets you construct a bijection X(B)→A(B,A)X(B)\to \mathscr A(B,A)X(B)→A(B,A) since for every x∈X(B)x\in X(B)x∈X(B) you get a unique arrow xˉ:B→A\bar{x}: B\to Axˉ:B→A such that X(xˉ)u=xX(\bar{x})u=xX(xˉ)u=x.

My problem is that I'm having a heck of a time seeing these bijections collectively are components of a natural transformation.

Denoting the components as ⋅ˉB\bar{\cdot}^B⋅ˉB (overline superscript B, this is hard to typeset...) I think I have to show that, for every f:B→B′f:B\to B'f:B→B′, that

(−∘f)∘⋅‾B′=⋅‾B∘Xf(-\circ f)\circ \overline{\cdot}^{B'} = \overline{\cdot}^B\circ Xf(−∘f)∘⋅B′=⋅B∘Xf

If you evaluate that at a y∈X(B′)y\in X(B')y∈X(B′) I think that looks like

y‾B′∘f=(Xf)y‾B\overline{y}^{B'}\circ f=\overline{(Xf)y}^By​B′∘f=(Xf)y​B

Couldn't make any headway from there... am I already mistaken?

Josselin Poiret (Nov 27 2024 at 10:09):

although the notation is probably a bit too complicated (one usually uses greek letters with subscripts for natural transformations), you've stated what needs to be proven. Now, remember how you defined the natural transformation at B B B: you get the unique arrow such that .... Can you exploit that to show that something is equal to that unique arrow such that ...?

Ryan Schwiebert (Nov 28 2024 at 14:07):

@Josselin Poiret Yes, that's good advice. I kept thinking and thinking and finally figured out what I was doing wrong. Let's use νB\nu_BνB​ for the overline notation instead.

I was trying to evaluate X(νB′(y)∘f)uX(\nu_{B'}(y)\circ f)uX(νB′​(y)∘f)u over and over again with no luck. Why? I forgot XXX is contravariant!

Once I remembered that, then I could finally show that both νB′(y)∘f\nu_{B'}(y)\circ fνB′​(y)∘f and νB((Xf)y)\nu_B((Xf)y)νB​((Xf)y) are the same map (since there can only be one you apply XXX to and evaluate at uuu to get (Xf)y(Xf)y(Xf)y.

Good exercise!

Ryan Schwiebert (Dec 19 2024 at 04:00):

Exercise 5.1.34 rearranges the diagram for an equalizer into a commutative square, and asks whether or not the square is a pullback square. Then conversely, it asks that if the square is a pullback, does that mean the upper left object is an equalizer

It seems pretty straightforward to check that if the square is a pullback square, then the upper left corner E is indeed an equalizer. But as for the first question, I think I made an ad-hoc category which shows the square can fail to be a pullback even if E is an equalizer.

IMG_0796.jpg

The ad hoc category is just the category for the graph drawn (objects E,P, A,B and arrows those depicted, along with identity arrows not depicted, all meaningful composite arrows, and subject to the listed constraints on arrows.)

Sanity check?

Josselin Poiret (Dec 19 2024 at 09:56):

do you really need α α α here? otherwise it seems fine but creating an ad-hoc category is often not the easiest solution for these kinds of exercises, as you need to check your claim on a somewhat complicated category

Josselin Poiret (Dec 19 2024 at 09:56):

I think here it will work fine, but you'd have to check that your generators and relations don't end up collapsing more stuff than you thought

Josselin Poiret (Dec 19 2024 at 09:57):

usually, a cleverly constructed diagram in Set suffices for this

Josselin Poiret (Dec 19 2024 at 10:00):

what I'd remark is that an equalizer in Set builds the subset of where two maps agree strictly, whereas a pullback can also choose how to make them agree up to a map on each side. You could then try to have two maps that don't agree strictly but could agree up to e.g. a permutation

Ryan Schwiebert (Dec 20 2024 at 00:49):

Josselin Poiret said:

do you really need α here?

I suppose not! I was focused on demonstrating that E wasn't the pullback object because something else was. But I suppose the mere existence of another object for the left hand corner of a commutative square, pullback or not, as long as it can't be mapped into E, then E couldn't be a pullback.

Yeah I'll be careful not to rely on ad-hoc categories that seem out of control. In this case, since the graph is cycle free and has a unique source and sink, it felt especially safe that there weren't any surprise arrows.

The only things I checked first were if some poset or group would work, but it seemed to me they would not work. Trying Set sounds like good advice, thank you. I will give it a think!

Ryan Schwiebert (Dec 20 2024 at 03:55):

I think if you take A=B={x,y}A=B=\{x,y\}A=B={x,y}, and you let sss be the identity map and ttt be the swap map, then the equalizer is empty, and the pullback has two elements.

That seems pretty easy... is that what you had in mind?

The two notions are still quite strange to me, especially in the category of sets. I'd like to think I understand kernels and quotients, but I guess I don't have my head wrapped around the more general notions yet.

For two arrows into an object B of set, the pullback is empty of the images of the arrows is disjoint, and the pullback is all of A×BA\times BA×B if the images coincide? It does sound like a measure of overlap...

The idea of where two maps agree is a lot easier to grasp right away. One thing that comes to mind for me like that, but is not obviously connected, is the notion of germs of functions at a base point (because it involves an equivalence relation where the functions have some region in common.)

Josselin Poiret (Dec 20 2024 at 14:24):

Ryan Schwiebert said:

That seems pretty easy... is that what you had in mind?

exactly! I would even call that set Bool \mathrm{Bool} Bool :)

Ryan Schwiebert (Jan 16 2025 at 03:51):

Comments on another exercise:
a) A category with all products and all equalizers also has all limits.
b) A category with binary products, terminal objects, and equalizers also has all finite limits

At first I thought the terminal object thing was some sort of nuance needed for getting all finite products from binary products. But it looks like it is solely to guarantee the empty product exists so one can say "all finite limits"? Does something go catastrophically wrong if you omit that or do you simply get "All finite limits on diagrams with one or more but finitely many objects"?

Secondly, it struck me that "product vs binary-product" is a thing but "binary-equalizer vs (general) equalizer" (as in, a limit cone on a diagram with two nodes but some family of arrows indexed by a set going one direction between the two nodes) was not considered. Glad to see I'm not the only one thinking about it.

Does the multi-equalizer just not come in very handy?

Kevin Carlson (Jan 16 2025 at 19:15):

That’s right, if you throw out the terminal object you’ll get a category with all non empty finite limits, which is a perfectly reasonable kind of thing. And I agree the multi-equalizer doesn’t seem to come up naturally all that much, although wide pullbacks are more common! In generally you can move between pullbacks and equalizers given the presence of products, and pullbacks are often easier to reason about or seem more fundamental. Perhaps this is because formally they are a simply connected limit shape.

Ryan Schwiebert (Jan 17 2025 at 02:49):

Kevin Carlson said:

simply connected

Interesting... I'd like to ask in what sense this is meant. I remember simply-connected topological spaces and it looks like there is some other sense in geometry, at least, too.

Kevin Carlson (Jan 17 2025 at 04:14):

Yes, it's related to simply connected spaces in that there is a (non-directed) loops in the diagram indexing pairs of parallel arrows, unlike in the diagram indexing pullbacks. In fact the definition is that a connected category is simply category if its geometric realization has trivial π1\pi_1π1​ in the sense of homotopy theory.

Ryan Schwiebert (Jan 24 2025 at 12:49):

5.2.22: a) For a set XXX and map f:X→Xf:X\to Xf:X→X describe the coequalizer of fff with 1X1_X1X​ as explicitly as possible.

This I think is not too bad. Thinking of the coequalizer as a quotient of X+XX+XX+X, I first said to myself "the relations given by the identity map glue these two copies together pointwise, so I may as well think of it as an equivalence relation on XXX. Is that on solid footing?

This relation joins elements with their images, but it both directions. To write that up, I decided to define C(S)=S∪f−1(S)∪f(S)C(S)=S\cup f^{-1}(S)\cup f(S)C(S)=S∪f−1(S)∪f(S) for each subset S⊆XS\subseteq XS⊆X, and then a typical equivalence class containing x∈Xx\in Xx∈X should look like ⋃i=1∞Ci({x})\bigcup_{i=1}^\infty C^i(\{x\})⋃i=1∞​Ci({x}).

That seems pretty explicit to me, but here comes part b):
b) Do the same in Top\mathrm{Top}Top. For XXX the unit circle, find an fff such that the coequalizer becomes an uncountable space with indiscrete topology.

My first impression is "uh... same thing?" But then I realize I don't know anything about the coproduct of topological spaces. A short wiki search later I have it (and am amazed I never ran into it in a topology course!) Now I have some more hope I can figure out if what I have in mind must have an indiscrete topology.

First problem is that I have the same problem with my strategy in Set: is the quotient space X⨿XX\amalg XX⨿X that describes the coproduct the same as the quotient space of XXX by the equivalence relation generated by x∼f(x)x\sim f(x)x∼f(x) after identifying the copies of XXX? And in that passage, is the topology any different than the one originally given to XXX? I guess it is not, but have to ask...

If the topology on XXX doesn't change in that identification, then it seems like I don't have to worry about the coproduct. All that remains is to make sure the quotient topology (which I did encounter in topology) has the indiscrete property.

My candidate is that fff can be some rotation of non-finite order. Since it's a homeomorphism of XXX to itself, the classes are not too messy. If the rotation spreads every point densely around the circle, then I can see why the topology has to be indiscrete.

So right now I'm mulling over how to prove the density bit. Feels like an undergraduate exercise...

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Jan 24 2025 at 13:35):

Ryan Schwiebert said:

5.2.22: a) For a set XXX and map f:X→Xf:X\to Xf:X→X describe the coequalizer of fff with 1X1_X1X​ as explicitly as possible.

This I think is not too bad. Thinking of the coequalizer as a quotient of X+XX+XX+X...

That doesn't sound right, it should be a quotient of XXX, or else you're confusing it with the pushout.

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Jan 24 2025 at 13:36):

(You end up building a quotient of X anyway, but you should have started there ;) )

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Jan 24 2025 at 13:43):

It sounds like you've got the correct idea for the circle example. Transformations like this are often called "ergodic", if you want something to look up to help with the density part (stronger properties than density hold)

Ryan Schwiebert (Jan 24 2025 at 19:20):

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

That doesn't sound right, it should be a quotient of X, or else you're confusing it with the pushout.

I don't think so... I was speaking of the construction of a general colimit via the coproduct and quotient as described in Leinster: Given a diagram D:I→AD:\mathcal I\to \mathscr AD:I→A, you start with the coproduct of the images of all objects in the image of DDD, and in the case of an equalizer diagram it's two objects.

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Jan 24 2025 at 20:37):

But the "quotient" that you take in that general construction is exactly the coequalizer of two maps!

Ryan Schwiebert (Jan 31 2025 at 12:46):

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

But the "quotient" that you take in that general construction is exactly the coequalizer of two maps!

Ok good? Isn't that what I set out to do?

On one hand I see what you mean in the wiki article, that the coequalizer in Set can be computed as an equivalence relation directly on the codomain of the pair. On the other hand, though, I think I'm faithfully executing the description of the coequalizer from coproduct and quotient. Maybe a case of potato potahto?

Or perhaps it just simplifies in this case because the diagram is so small: AAA is the only source, so it winds up getting eliminated.

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Jan 31 2025 at 14:59):

Right, it seems like you were doing the right calculation. I reacted as I did because the result you're referring to states that you can construct any colimit as soon as you have coproducts and coequalizers (the dual result for limits is the more commonly stated version), and to apply that result you need to be able to construct coequalizers in the first place.

Last updated: Feb 01 2025 at 12:07 UTC

This archive runs on a customization of zulip-archive
